disease.name: Gastric Outlet Obstruction
disease.note: Mechanical obstruction at the gastric outlet (pylorus or duodenum). Presents with vomiting of undigested food hours after meals, weight loss, and epigastric distension with succussion splash. In Kenya, benign causes (peptic ulcer disease, H. pylori) are common but malignant causes (gastric cancer) must be excluded, especially in older patients.
presentingComplaints: Feeling of fullness after small meals
Weight loss from inability to eat
pathophysiologyDetail: Mechanical obstruction at pylorus or duodenum: narrowing from benign peptic scarring, malignant infiltration, or extrinsic compression prevents passage of gastric contents into duodenum

Peptic stricture pathophysiology: chronic H. pylori infection or NSAID use → duodenal/pyloric channel ulcer → repeated cycles of ulceration and healing → fibrous scar formation → concentric luminal narrowing

Malignant obstruction: gastric antral adenocarcinoma infiltrates and constricts the pyloric channel; pancreatic head adenocarcinoma compresses the duodenum externally; both cause progressive, unremitting obstruction
Gastric dilatation: chronic outlet obstruction → stomach dilates to accommodate retained contents → loss of smooth muscle tone → irreversible atony if obstruction persists >2-3 weeks

Hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis: loss of gastric HCl through vomiting → volume contraction → renal compensation (increased HCO3 reabsorption) → K+ depletion from aldosterone-mediated renal K+ wasting

Paradoxical aciduria: severe metabolic alkalosis → kidneys excrete alkaline urine initially, then with profound K+ depletion → H+ moves intracellularly → urine becomes acidic (paradoxical aciduria — a key diagnostic clue)
Volume depletion cascade: vomiting → total body fluid depletion → prerenal azotemia → compensatory renin-angiotensin-aldosterone activation → worsens K+ loss → perpetuates alkalosis
Tetany and neuromuscular irritability: metabolic alkalosis decreases ionized calcium fraction → Chvostek sign, Trousseau sign, carpopedal spasm, seizures in severe cases
Aspiration pneumonitis: regurgitation of stagnant gastric contents → chemical pneumonitis (Mendelson syndrome) especially during sleep or altered consciousness
Gastric stasis and bacterial overgrowth: retained food in dilated stomach → fermentation by anaerobic bacteria → foul-smelling breath (halitosis) and morning vomiting of sour material
Weight loss and malnutrition: inability to eat adequate calories → protein-calorie malnutrition → hypoalbuminemia → immunosuppression → poor surgical outcomes
Mallory-Weiss tears: repeated forceful retching/vomiting → mucosal tears at gastroesophageal junction → hematemesis and melena
Gastric rupture/ perforation: massive gastric dilatation with ischemia → necrosis of gastric wall → perforation → peritonitis — a surgical emergency with high mortality
Bezoar formation: retained undigested food fibers → phytobezoars (vegetable matter) or trichobezoars (hair) → worsen obstruction and may cause gastric ulceration
Biochemical adaptation: renal excretion of HCO3 increases with volume repletion; rapid correction of alkalosis can unmask hypocalcemia and cause tetany

East African context: benign causes (H. pylori, NSAID overuse) dominate in younger patients; malignant GOO from gastric cancer is common in patients >50; late presentation with severe metabolic derangement is typical due to limited healthcare access

landmarkTrials.population: 120 patients with malignant gastric outlet obstruction
landmarkTrials.intervention: Self-expanding metallic stent (SEMS) vs palliative gastrojejunostomy
landmarkTrials.outcome: Stent group: faster oral intake (4 vs 8 days, p<0.01), shorter hospital stay, similar overall survival. Gastrojejunostomy: better long-term patency, fewer reinterventions. Stents preferred for life expectancy <3 months.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Stents costly and not widely available in Kenyan public hospitals; surgical gastrojejunostomy remains primary approach

landmarkTrials.trial: Dutch GOO Trial (2007, Annals of Surgery)
landmarkTrials.population: 105 patients with malignant GOO — meta-analysis of 10 comparative studies
landmarkTrials.intervention: Stent vs gastrojejunostomy for malignant gastric outlet obstruction
landmarkTrials.outcome: Stent: shorter time to oral intake (mean 4 vs 10 days), shorter hospital stay, but higher recurrence rate (18% vs 2%) and more reinterventions. No difference in survival or overall complication rates.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Both options considered; gastrojejunostomy more available in Kenya

landmarkTrials.trial: Endoscopic Balloon Dilation for Benign GOO (Kochman et al, 2000, Gastrointestinal Endoscopy)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Through-the-scope balloon dilation (15-20 mm balloon diameter)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Immediate technical success 95%, long-term (12-month) symptom relief in 67%. H. pylori eradication improved outcomes (83% vs 55% without eradication). Need for repeat dilation in 30%.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Balloon dilation feasible in Kenyan endoscopy units; reusable balloons reduce costs

landmarkTrials.trial: Malignant GOO: 20 mm vs 22 mm Stents (Kim et al, 2010, Surgical Endoscopy)
landmarkTrials.population: 156 patients with malignant GOO randomized to 20 mm vs 22 mm SEMS
landmarkTrials.intervention: Endoscopic placement of uncovered SEMS (20 mm vs 22 mm diameter)
landmarkTrials.outcome: 22 mm stents had lower migration rate (4% vs 14%), better oral intake score at 4 weeks, and longer stent patency (median 120 vs 90 days). No difference in complication rates.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Where stents available, larger diameter stents preferred; cost remains barrier

landmarkTrials.trial: H. pylori Eradication in Pyloric Stenosis (Lam et al, 2000, Alimentary Pharmacology & Therapeutics)
landmarkTrials.population: 42 patients with H. pylori-positive duodenal ulcer complicated by pyloric stenosis
landmarkTrials.intervention: H. pylori eradication treatment + PPI vs PPI alone after balloon dilation
landmarkTrials.outcome: Eradication group: 96% recurrence-free at 12 months vs 62% in PPI-only group. H. pylori eradication dramatically reduced need for repeat dilation.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Strongly relevant — H. pylori cure is cheap and widely available, crucial first step

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic vs Open Gastrojejunostomy (Nakamura et al, 2014, Surgical Endoscopy)
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of 8 studies comparing laparoscopic vs open gastrojejunostomy
landmarkTrials.outcome: Laparoscopic approach: lower blood loss, less pain, shorter hospital stay (5 vs 9 days, p<0.01), earlier oral intake. Equivalent anastomotic leak rates (3-5%) and survival.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Laparoscopic surgery growing in Kenyan tertiary centers; open remains standard at district level

landmarkTrials.trial: GOO-SEMS vs Duodenal Stent Comparative Study (Park et al, 2015, Surgical Endoscopy)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Partially covered vs uncovered SEMS for malignant GOO
landmarkTrials.outcome: Covered stents: less tumor ingrowth (12% vs 28%) but higher migration rate (20% vs 6%). Uncovered stents: better long-term patency. No difference in oral intake or survival.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Uncovered stents preferred in resource-limited settings due to lower migration

evidenceTable.therapy: SEMS vs Gastrojejunostomy for malignant GOO (Systematic Review)
evidenceTable.endpoint: Time to oral intake and patency
evidenceTable.effect: Stent: oral intake 4 days; GJ: 8 days. Stent reintervention 18% vs GJ 2%
evidenceTable.recommendation: Stent for limited prognosis (<3 months); GJ for better long-term palliation

evidenceTable.therapy: Endoscopic balloon dilation for benign pyloric stenosis
evidenceTable.population: Benign pyloric stenosis from peptic ulcer disease
evidenceTable.effect: 67% symptom-free at 12 months; 83% with H. pylori eradication
evidenceTable.recommendation: First-line endoscopic therapy for benign GOO

evidenceTable.therapy: H. pylori eradication + PPI vs PPI alone in pyloric stenosis
evidenceTable.population: H. pylori-positive duodenal ulcer with pyloric stenosis
evidenceTable.recommendation: Mandatory H. pylori testing and eradication in benign GOO

evidenceTable.effect: Lap: 5 days, Open: 9 days; equivalent leak rates (3-5%)
evidenceTable.recommendation: Laparoscopic approach preferred when expertise available

evidenceTable.population: Benign pyloric stenosis refractory to dilation
evidenceTable.recommendation: Surgical option after failed endoscopic therapy for benign disease

evidenceTable.therapy: Covered vs Uncovered SEMS for malignant GOO
evidenceTable.effect: Covered: less ingrowth (12% vs 28%), more migration (20% vs 6%)
evidenceTable.recommendation: Uncovered stents preferred for malignant GOO

evidenceTable.therapy: IV PPI vs No PPI in ulcer healing post-dilation
evidenceTable.effect: PPI reduces recurrence by ~50% compared to no acid suppression
evidenceTable.recommendation: All patients with benign GOO should receive PPI therapy

evidenceTable.effect: Immediate symptom relief; reduces aspiration risk; facilitates gastric lavage before endoscopy
evidenceTable.recommendation: NG decompression is first-line urgent management

evidenceTable.therapy: Surgical drainage: Truncal vagotomy + drainage for benign GOO
evidenceTable.population: Benign GOO with acid hypersecretion (pre-PPI era, historical)
evidenceTable.effect: Truncal vagotomy + drainage (gastrojejunostomy/pyloroplasty): ulcer recurrence 5-10% at 5 years
evidenceTable.recommendation: Now replaced by H. pylori eradication + PPI + endoscopic dilation; surgery reserved for failures

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical diagnosis — vomiting of undigested food >4 hours after meals, weight loss, epigastric fullness ± visible gastric peristalsis, succussion splash on bedside examination

Step 2: NG tube placement — large bore (14-16 Fr) inserted for decompression; residual volume >200 mL suggests obstruction; >1000 mL indicates severe obstruction; character of aspirate (undigested food vs bilious) guides level
Step 3: Laboratory evaluation — electrolytes (Na, K, Cl, HCO3), blood gas (ABG/VBG), CBC, urea/creatinine, albumin. Hallmark: hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis with prerenal azotemia

Step 4: Upper GI endoscopy — gold standard diagnostic test. Identify location (pylorus vs duodenal), cause (ulcer, stricture, mass), and tissue diagnosis if malignant. Assess ability to pass scope through obstruction

Step 5: CT abdomen with IV and oral contrast — essential if malignancy suspected. Assess gastric wall thickness, antral mass, pancreatic head mass, duodenal pathology, peritoneal disease, and distant metastases for staging

Step 6: Upper GI barium meal — if endoscopy unavailable or incomplete. Shows level of obstruction, gastric dilatation, and mucosal pattern. 'Beak sign' at pylorus suggests malignant stricture; smooth tapering suggests benign

Step 7: Distinguish benign vs malignant — endoscopy + biopsy + CT essential. Malignant: >50 years, short symptom duration, palpable mass, severe weight loss, Virchow's node. Benign: younger, long-standing ulcer history, H. pylori positive

Step 8: Therapeutic plan — benign: endoscopic balloon dilation ± H. pylori eradication + PPI. Malignant: staging → resectability assessment → curative resection vs palliative surgery vs SEMS vs chemotherapy

Step 9: Nutritional assessment — albumin, prealbumin, weight loss percentage. Consider nasojejunal feeding beyond obstruction or parenteral nutrition if severely malnourished before definitive treatment
Step 10: Multidisciplinary approach — surgeon, gastroenterologist, oncologist, nutritionist. Goal: relieve obstruction, correct metabolic derangement, treat underlying cause

Kenya adaptation: In district hospitals without endoscopy, NG decompression + IV fluids + IV PPI as initial management; refer to provincial hospital for endoscopy; clinical response to PPI supports benign etiology
managementPearls: Do not rush to surgery in benign GOO — correct volume depletion, electrolytes, and alkalosis first; the patient is at highest risk from metabolic derangement, not the obstruction itself
Hypokalemic hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis requires aggressive IV Normal Saline + KCl (20-40 mEq/L); do NOT use Ringer's Lactate (contains no K+ replacement) and avoid NaHCO3 (worsens alkalosis)

NG tube decompression must precede endoscopy in complete obstruction — retained food obscures endoscopic view and increases aspiration risk; large-bore (14-16 Fr) tube with lavage until returns are clear
H. pylori eradication is the single most effective long-term therapy for benign GOO — cure rates >90% with 14-day triple therapy (amoxicillin 1 g + clarithromycin 500 mg + PPI twice daily)
Endoscopic balloon dilation: start with 15 mm balloon, gradually increase to 18-20 mm over 2-3 sessions 1-2 weeks apart; aggressive single dilation >20 mm risks perforation (rate 3-5%)
Malignant GOO: self-expanding metallic stents allow oral intake within 24 hours and hospital discharge in 2-3 days — ideal for patients with life expectancy <3 months or poor surgical candidates
Surgical gastrojejunostomy remains the gold standard for malignant GOO with good performance status and life expectancy >6 months — better long-term patency than stents with fewer reinterventions
Laparoscopic gastrojejunostomy is preferred over open when expertise exists — reduced pain, faster recovery, earlier oral intake, and shorter hospital stay with equivalent outcomes
Retrocolic gastrojejunostomy has lower incidence of bile reflux and marginal ulceration compared to antecolic; but antecolic is simpler and avoids Petersen's defect herniation risk

In patients with metastatic gastric cancer causing GOO, palliative gastrojejunostomy should be considered only if ECOG 0-1 — poor outcomes in ECOG ≥2 patients, where SEMS or BSC may be more appropriate

Post-pyloroplasty dumping syndrome occurs in 10-15% — warn patients about early dumping (palpitations, sweating after meals) and late dumping (hypoglycemia 1-3 hours postprandial); small, frequent, low-sugar meals

H. pylori-negative benign GOO is increasingly common in Kenya due to prior antibiotic exposure — consider NSAID-induced stricture; test with stool antigen (more sensitive than serology post-treatment)
operativeNuances: Laparoscopic gastrojejunostomy: place patient in supine split-leg position with reverse Trendelenburg; 5-port configuration (12 mm umbilical camera, 5 mm bilateral subcostal, 5 mm left flank); 30° laparoscope essential for visualization
Select the most dependent portion of the gastric body (not antrum — too prone to reflux) for the anastomosis; mark with methylene blue if needed to confirm dependent drainage

Retrocolic technique: create a generous window in the avascular portion of the transverse mesocolon to the left of the middle colic vessels; exteriorize proximal jejunum through this window without tension or torsion

Antecolic technique: bring jejunal loop anterior to transverse colon and omentum; simpler but associated with higher marginal ulcer rates and bile reflux; leave at least 40-50 cm of afferent limb to reduce reflux

Hand-sewn gastrojejunostomy: two-layer closure — inner full-thickness continuous 3-0 Vicryl or PDS, outer Lambert seromuscular interrupted 3-0 silk; create 4 cm stoma (approximately two finger-breadths)

Stapled gastrojejunostomy: create gastrotomy and enterotomy; fire 45 mm linear stapler ×2 for adequate stoma width; close common enterotomy with running 3-0 absorbable suture; check patency with Babcock clamp

Heineke-Mikulicz pyloroplasty: longitudinal incision across pylorus (2 cm gastric × 2 cm duodenal), transverse closure with interrupted 3-0 absorbable sutures — full-thickness, carefully approximating mucosa
In East Africa where stapling devices may be unavailable or prohibitively expensive, hand-sewn anastomosis with polyglactin (Vicryl) or polydioxanone (PDS) is safe and well-established
Petersen's space defect (between Roux limb and transverse mesocolon) must be closed with non-absorbable suture to prevent internal hernia — a potentially fatal complication easily prevented

Feeding jejunostomy should be placed at the time of gastrojejunostomy in any malnourished patient (albumin <3.0, weight loss >10%) — allows enteral nutrition if anastomotic failure or delayed gastric emptying occurs

prognosis.benignCompleteResolution: >90% of benign GOO resolves with H. pylori eradication + endoscopic dilation + PPI; <10% require surgical drainage
prognosis.benignDilationRecurrence: 30-40% require repeat dilation within 12 months; H. pylori eradication reduces recurrence rate from 38% to 4% (Lam et al 2000)
prognosis.malignantUnresectable: Median survival 3-6 months for unresectable malignant GOO; SEMS or gastrojejunostomy improves quality of life but does not extend survival
prognosis.malignantResectable: Survival determined by underlying malignancy (gastric or pancreatic cancer); 5-year survival 20-60% depending on stage
prognosticFactors: Cause of obstruction (benign vs malignant) — single most important determinant of long-term prognosis
Nutritional status (albumin >3.5 g/dL predicts better outcomes; albumin <2.5 g/dL predicts poor surgical recovery)
ECOG performance status — ECOG 0-1 tolerate surgery well; ECOG ≥2 have significantly higher complication rates
Severity of metabolic derangement on presentation (pH >7.55, K+ <2.5 associated with worse outcomes)
Symptom duration — acute <2 weeks suggests malignancy; chronic >3 months suggests benign etiology
prognosis.eastAfricanSpecific: Benign GOO in Kenya often linked to untreated H. pylori and NSAID overuse from traditional pain remedies; malignant GOO usually presents late with advanced inoperable disease; limited stent availability means surgical drainage remains primary palliative modality

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Severe metabolic disturbance/perforation -> SEVERE; chronic partial obstruction -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Do you vomit food that you ate several hours earlier?
Is the vomit undigested and does it have a sour smell?
Have you lost weight because of vomiting?
Do you feel full after eating only a small amount?
Do you have a gurgling sensation in your upper abdomen?
Have you had stomach ulcers in the past?
Have you taken traditional medicines for stomach pain?
Do you use ibuprofen, aspirin, or painkillers regularly?
Do you smoke cigarettes?
Do you drink alcohol — chang'aa, busaa, or beer?
Have you had an endoscopy before?
Has anyone in your family had stomach cancer?
Do you have difficulty swallowing food as well?
Is the vomiting more at night or after meals?
Do you feel better after vomiting?
Have you noticed black stools or blood in vomit?
Do you have burning epigastric pain relieved by food?
Are you diabetic?
Do you vomit undigested food from meals eaten 6-12 hours earlier?
Is the vomit large volume (>500 mL) and foul-smelling?
Do you feel a gurgling or splashing sensation in your abdomen when you move?
Have you had surgery on your stomach before?
Were you ever treated for H. pylori infection?
Do you take traditional herbal medicines for abdominal pain?
Have you had a prior endoscopy that showed pyloric stenosis?
Do you eat a lot of dried maize, beans, or fibrous vegetables? (bezoar risk)
Have you ever swallowed something that could block your stomach? (bezoar)
Do you have heartburn or acid reflux as well?
Is the pain better or worse after vomiting? (better = gastric outlet obstruction, worse = perforation)
Have you noticed muscle cramps, tingling in your fingers, or tetany? (hypocalcemia from alkalosis)
Do you feel your heart racing or fluttering? (arrhythmia from hypokalemia)
Has a doctor ever told you that your blood potassium or pH is abnormal?
Do you have difficulty swallowing food or liquids? (differentiates gastric from esophageal)
Have you had a stroke or neurological condition? (gastroparesis differential)
Do you have episodes of abdominal pain so severe you cannot move? (perforation concern)
Have you coughed up or vomited blood?
What is your occupation and daily diet? (nutritional assessment for recovery planning)

examination_findings.finding: Epigastric distension — visible fullness in upper abdomen
Succussion splash — splashing sound on shaking abdomen (pathognomonic)
Visible gastric peristalsis — waves across epigastrium
Epigastric tenderness — variable
Palpable epigastric mass — suggests malignancy
Signs of dehydration — dry oral mucosa, reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes
Cachexia and muscle wasting in chronic obstruction
Halitosis — foul breath from fermented gastric contents
Tachycardia from dehydration or anemia
Pallor from iron deficiency anemia (malignancy or chronic disease)
Signs of hypokalemia — muscle weakness, hyporeflexia
Left supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow's node) if malignant
Hepatomegaly if metastatic
Ascites if peritoneal carcinomatosis
Rectal Blumer's shelf on DRE if advanced malignancy
Visible epigastric distension — dome-shaped upper abdomen even when supine
Gastric peristaltic waves visible across epigastrium (from left to right traveling)
Succussion splash — most reliable sign; shake epigastrium and auscultate for splash sound
Halitosis (fetid breath) from fermented gastric contents — detectable at bedside
Signs of hypokalemia: muscle weakness (proximal > distal), hyporeflexia, positive Chvostek sign
Signs of hypocalcemia: carpopedal spasm, Trousseau sign, perioral tingling
Trousseau sign (carpopedal spasm with BP cuff inflation) — unmasked by alkalosis-induced hypocalcemia
Orthostatic hypotension (BP drop >20 mmHg standing) from volume depletion
Sunken eyes, dry oral mucosa, reduced skin turgor, delayed capillary refill — dehydration signs
Tachycardia and low-volume pulse from hypovolemia
Chest crackles or wheezes from aspiration pneumonitis
Guarding/rigidity if perforation has occurred — generalized peritonitis
Liver dullness percussion — if absent, suspect pneumoperitoneum from perforation
Left supraclavicular node (Virchow's) firm and fixed suggests malignancy
Epigastric mass — firm, irregular, non-tender suggests antral carcinoma
Palpable pancreatic mass if pancreatic head cancer (Courvoisier sign + if jaundiced)
Ascites (shifting dullness) from peritoneal carcinomatosis in advanced malignancy
Icterus/jaundice if malignant obstruction from pancreatic head mass compressing bile duct

bedside_scores.name: Gastric Outlet Obstruction Scoring System (GOOSS)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score <2 indicates significant obstruction needing intervention

riskFactors.note: Chronic duodenal or pyloric channel ulcers lead to scarring
Head of pancreas tumors compress duodenum
Leading cause of peptic ulcer disease

subtypes.name: Benign Gastric Outlet Obstruction
subtypes.management: NG tube decompression. Correction of dehydration and electrolyte imbalance (IV Normal Saline + KCl 20-40 mEq/L). IV PPI. Endoscopic balloon dilatation (15-20 mm balloon). H. pylori eradication if positive. Surgical drainage (pyloroplasty, gastrojejunostomy) if endoscopic therapy fails.

subtypes.name: Malignant Gastric Outlet Obstruction
subtypes.management: Staging CT. Palliative gastrojejunostomy (open or laparoscopic). Self-expanding metallic stent (SEMS) for inoperable cases — faster recovery but higher reobstruction rate. Chemotherapy if metastatic. Nutritional support.

mimics.whyMimic: Delayed gastric emptying without mechanical obstruction
Duodenal obstruction at the level of SMA crossing
External compression causing gastric outlet obstruction
Gastric outlet obstruction after corrosive ingestion
Epigastric distension, vomiting, inability to pass NG tube
Epigastric mass, early satiety, vomiting, gastric outlet obstruction
Gastric wall thickening, vomiting, early satiety, can cause obstructive symptoms
Chronic vomiting, abdominal distension, delayed gastric emptying without mechanical block
Duodenal obstruction presenting in adults with vomiting and epigastric fullness
Large duodenal diverticulum can compress duodenum causing outlet obstruction

mimics.diseaseName: Diffuse Gastric Cancer (Linitis Plastica) — Mimics Benign GOO
mimics.whyMimic: Can present with early satiety, vomiting, no discrete mass on imaging

bedside.name: NG tube placement + residual volume measurement
bedside.indication: Gastric residual >200 mL suggests obstruction; >1000 mL indicates severe obstruction

laboratory.name: CBC
laboratory.indication: Hemoconcentration from dehydration, anemia from chronic disease

laboratory.name: Albumin
laboratory.indication: Nutritional status — low albumin suggests chronicity/malignancy

imaging.name: Upper GI Endoscopy (EGD)
imaging.indication: Identify cause (stenosis, mass, ulcer), biopsy, therapeutic dilation

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV + oral contrast
imaging.indication: Assess for malignant cause, tumor staging, metastases

imaging.name: Upper GI barium meal
imaging.indication: Demonstrate level of obstruction, gastric dilatation (when endoscopy not available)

imaging.name: Endoscopic Ultrasound (EUS)
imaging.indication: Assess wall layers, extraluminal compression, tissue sampling

management.protocolReference: ASGE Guidelines for Gastric Outlet Obstruction (2021)

resuscitation: NG tube placement on free drainage — large bore (14-16 Fr) for gastric decompression
IV Normal Saline 1-2 L stat, then 125-150 mL/hr
Potassium replacement: add KCl 20-40 mEq/L to IV fluids — correct hypokalemia aggressively
Monitor electrolytes and blood gas every 6-12 hours until normalized
Urinary catheter — monitor output, target >0.5 mL/kg/hr
analgesia: Avoid morphine until diagnosis confirmed (risk of aspiration)
conservative: IV PPI (Omeprazole 40 mg IV daily or Pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily)
H. pylori testing and eradication if positive

surgical.indication: Failed endoscopic therapy, malignant obstruction with good performance status
surgical.step2: Identify ligament of Treitz, select proximal jejunal loop
surgical.step6: Alternative: Heineke-Mikulicz pyloroplasty for benign strictures

nonOperative: Endoscopic balloon dilation: 15-20 mm balloon, serial dilations over weeks
Self-expanding metallic stent (SEMS): for malignant GOO — allows oral intake within 24h
Duodenal stent for malignant duodenal obstruction

general: NG tube on free drainage — remove when output <200 mL/day (typically day 2-3)
IV fluids until tolerating oral intake
PPI IV for 48 hours then oral
diet_advancement: Day 3-5: Semisolid diet (porridge, yoghurt, mashed potatoes)
Lifelong: small frequent meals if gastrojejunostomy
discharge_criteria: NG tube removed and no abdominal distension

pathways.criteria: Peptic ulcer etiology, no malignancy, partial obstruction
pathways.treatment: Decompression with NG tube. IV fluids with KCl and electrolyte correction. IV PPI. Endoscopic balloon dilatation. H. pylori eradication. Surgical drainage if fails.
Staging CT. Curative resection (gastrectomy, Whipple) if possible. Palliative gastrojejunostomy if unresectable. Endoscopic SEMS if poor surgical candidate.

operative_templates.indication: Gastric outlet obstruction — malignant or benign refractory to endoscopic therapy
steps: Assess stomach, pylorus, duodenum, and pancreas
Select proximal jejunal loop (30-40 cm from ligament of Treitz)
Create window in transverse mesocolon (retrocolic approach)
Bring jejunal loop through window to stomach
Mark anastomosis site on posterior gastric wall (dependent part)
Stapled anastomosis using 45 mm linear stapler ×2
Close enterotomy defect with 3-0 absorbable suture
Close Petersen's defect (between Roux limb and transverse mesocolon)

dischargeCriteria: Tolerating semisolid diet without vomiting for 24 hours
NG tube removed and abdomen soft, not distended
Nutritional plan in place — small frequent meals

patient_education.explanation: Gastric outlet obstruction means the opening from your stomach to your small intestine (the pylorus) is narrowed or blocked. This prevents food from passing through, causing vomiting, weight loss, and dehydration. The cause may be a healed ulcer (benign) or a tumor (malignant). Treatment relieves the blockage with balloon dilation, a metal stent, or surgery to create a new connection (gastrojejunostomy).
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital if: you cannot keep any fluids down, you develop severe abdominal pain, you vomit blood or coffee-ground material, you have a fever with chills, or your abdomen becomes very swollen and tender.
patient_education.activity: After surgery, take gentle walks. Avoid heavy lifting for 4-6 weeks. Sleep with head elevated to prevent reflux. You can resume normal activities in 2-4 weeks after full recovery.
patient_education.diet: Eat small, frequent meals (6-8 per day). Chew food very well. Eat slowly. Do not drink large amounts with meals. Avoid foods that are hard to digest: tough meat, raw vegetables, nuts, seeds. Good foods: porridge (uji), soup, yoghurt, mashed potatoes, bananas, eggs, soft rice, well-cooked vegetables.
patient_education.medication: Take omeprazole or pantoprazole as prescribed to reduce stomach acid. Take iron supplements and vitamin B12 if recommended. For malignant cases, follow chemotherapy plan as discussed.
patient_education.followUp: Return to clinic at 2 weeks for wound check. At 6 weeks for symptom assessment. Repeat endoscopy may be needed. For benign cases: ensure H. pylori has been eradicated. For malignant cases: oncology follow-up.

before_discharge: Educate patient on small frequent meals and eating slowly
If H. pylori positive: confirm eradication therapy prescription

after_discharge.action: Surgical clinic review — wound check, diet tolerance assessment
Clinical assessment — symptom resolution, weight trend
Repeat endoscopy if stent placed or dilation performed; check stent patency

when_to_return: Vomiting or inability to tolerate oral intake
Vomiting blood or passing black stools
